content.text: A DPIA template for GenAI is a structured document that guides an organization through the four mandatory components of a Data Protection Impact Assessment under GDPR Article 35: a systematic description of the processing operations, an assessment of the necessity and proportionality of the processing, an assessment of the risks to data subjects, and the measures envisaged to address those risks. The template must be adapted for GenAI-specific risks that do not appear in traditional software DPIAs.

Risk mitigation in the context of a GenAI DPIA means identifying controls that reduce the likelihood or impact of identified risks to a level that is acceptable to the data controller and the Data Protection Officer. Where residual risks remain high despite mitigation, GDPR Article 36 requires prior consultation with the supervisory authority before processing begins.

The DPIA template is the core artifact of privacy-by-design for GenAI. Unlike a trigger assessment, which is a screening step, the full DPIA documents the actual processing, articulates the specific risks to individuals, and records the controls put in place to address them. Regulators examining a data protection incident involving an AI system will ask for the DPIA; a missing, incomplete, or generic DPIA that does not address AI-specific risks is evidence of inadequate governance.

GenAI systems have risk categories that standard DPIA templates do not cover: the possibility that personal data provided in prompts is used to train or fine-tune the model, the risk that AI outputs hallucinate personal information about real individuals, the risk that automated decisions based on AI outputs violate GDPR Article 22 rights, and the risk that prompt injection attacks can cause the system to exfiltrate personal data. A template that does not include these categories produces incomplete risk assessments.
Provides a consistent, repeatable structure that ensures all mandatory GDPR Article 35 components are addressed
Forces cross-functional collaboration by requiring input from engineering (data flows), legal (lawful basis), and security (risk identification)
Creates the documented evidence of privacy-by-design required by GDPR Article 25
Identifies risk mitigation opportunities early in the design process when changes are cheapest to implement
Produces the artifact required for DPO sign-off and, when necessary, supervisory authority consultation
Serves as a living document that is updated when the system changes, maintaining an accurate risk record over the system's lifecycle
A DPIA template comprehensive enough to cover all GenAI risk categories may be too long for engineering teams to complete without DPO guidance
A generic template can be reused across systems but may not capture the specific nuances of each deployment
Treating the DPIA as a one-time artifact rather than updating it when the system changes creates a growing gap between the documented and actual risk profile
Involving the DPO only at sign-off misses the opportunity for DPO guidance during risk identification that could lead to better-designed mitigations

The DPIA process for a GenAI system proceeds through four sections, each building on the previous. The full assessment should be drafted by the product and engineering team with DPO guidance and reviewed by Legal before DPO sign-off.

The first section provides a comprehensive description of the processing operations. For a GenAI system, this includes: the purpose of the processing and the specific tasks the AI performs; the categories of personal data processed including prompt content, retrieved documents, generated outputs, and logs; the data flows showing how data moves between users, the AI API or model, and any downstream systems; the actors involved including data controllers, processors, and sub-processors; the data subject populations affected; and the retention periods for all categories of data.

The data flow diagram is the most important artifact in this section. For GenAI systems, data flows are often more complex than for traditional software because data may pass through: the user interface, an orchestration layer, a vector database, an LLM API operated by a third-party provider, tool calls to external services, and output processing layers, before reaching the user. Each of these hops must be documented with the data types that cross each boundary.

The second section assesses whether the processing is necessary for the stated purpose and proportionate to the risks it creates. This section covers: the lawful basis for processing under GDPR Article 6 (and Article 9 for special-category data), whether the purpose could be achieved with less personal data (data minimization), whether the data subjects have been informed through privacy notices, whether they have meaningful rights (access, correction, deletion, objection), and whether the retention periods are the minimum necessary.

For GenAI systems, the necessity and proportionality assessment must specifically address: whether it is necessary for the AI to process personal data at the level of granularity proposed, whether de-identification or pseudonymization before the AI processes the data is feasible, and whether automated decision-making is necessary or whether human review can achieve the same outcome with lower risk to data subjects.

The third section identifies and scores the specific risks to data subjects. Risk scoring uses a two-axis matrix: likelihood (how probable is it that this risk materializes?) and impact (how severe would the harm be if it does?). The combination produces a risk level: low, medium, high, or critical. Risks scoring high or critical require mitigation measures; risks remaining high or critical after mitigation trigger a supervisory authority consultation obligation under Article 36.

GenAI-specific risk categories that must be addressed include: unauthorized access to or leakage of personal data provided in prompts, hallucination producing false information about identifiable individuals that could damage their reputation, automated decision-making affecting individuals without meaningful human review, discriminatory outputs reflecting biases in training data, prompt injection attacks causing the system to exfiltrate personal data, re-identification of nominally anonymized data through model outputs, and scope creep where data collected for one purpose is repurposed through the AI system for another.

The fourth section documents the technical and organizational measures that reduce each identified risk. For each risk, the assessment records the proposed mitigation, the expected risk reduction after mitigation, and the residual risk level. Where residual risk remains high, the assessment notes the DPO's review and either the DPO's acceptance of the residual risk or the obligation to consult the supervisory authority.

attrs.alt: DPIA Template Structure and Risk Scoring for GenAI

content.text: The diagram shows the four-section DPIA structure at the top, the risk scoring matrix on the lower left, GenAI-specific risk categories in the center, and corresponding mitigation controls on the right. Critical and high residual risks trigger DPO review and potentially supervisory authority consultation.
Immediately after the trigger assessment determines a DPIA is mandatory, before development begins
When updating an existing DPIA following a material change to the GenAI system such as adding a new data source, changing the model provider, or expanding automated decision-making
During annual DPIA review cycles for ongoing GenAI systems
When a data breach involving the GenAI system reveals that the original DPIA did not adequately account for the breach scenario

Adapt the four-section GDPR DPIA structure to your GenAI context. Add a GenAI-specific risk register pre-populated with the risk categories listed in Section 3 above so assessors do not miss AI-specific risks. Require a data flow diagram as a mandatory artifact in Section 1.

Assign a DPIA owner, typically the product manager or a privacy engineer, who is responsible for driving the assessment to completion. The DPO provides guidance throughout, not just at sign-off. Security contributes to risk identification. Legal reviews the lawful basis and necessity assessment. Engineering validates the data flow description.

Schedule a DPO consultation at the beginning of the DPIA to agree on scope and methodology, at the risk identification stage to ensure completeness, and at the end for formal sign-off. Early DPO engagement is more valuable than a late review that identifies problems requiring design rework.

## DPIA Template — Section 3: GenAI Risk Register

| Risk ID | Risk Description | Likelihood | Impact | Initial Risk | Mitigation Measure | Residual Risk |
|---------|-----------------|-----------|--------|-------------|-------------------|--------------|
| R01 | Personal data in prompts accessed by third-party model provider beyond contractual scope | Medium | High | High | Data Processing Agreement with model provider; contractual prohibition on training use; audit right | Medium |
| R02 | Hallucination: AI generates false personal information about real individuals | High | Medium | High | Human review required before external use of AI output; disclaimer on outputs; no auto-publish | Medium |
| R03 | Automated decision made using AI output without Art.22 safeguards | Medium | High | High | Mandatory human review gate before any consequential decision; documented escalation path | Low |
| R04 | Prompt injection causes AI to exfiltrate personal data to attacker | Low | Critical | High | Input sanitization; output filtering; rate limiting; anomaly detection on unusual data in outputs | Medium |
| R05 | AI output reveals training data containing personal information (memorization) | Low | High | Medium | Use of API-based model with no training on customer data; DPA clause prohibiting training use | Low |
| R06 | Discriminatory output affects protected characteristics (Art.22 + Equality law) | Medium | High | High | Regular bias testing; demographic parity monitoring; human review for consequential decisions | Medium |
| R07 | Scope creep: data collected for purpose A repurposed through AI for purpose B | Low | High | Medium | Purpose limitation enforcement in system design; DPO review required for new use cases | Low |
| R08 | Excessive retention of personal data in AI conversation logs | Medium | Medium | Medium | Log retention policy max 30 days; automated deletion; no personal data in training data pipeline | Low |

# Section 1: Data Flow Description (YAML format for machine-readable documentation)
genai_system:
  name: "Clinical Documentation Assistant"
  version: "1.0"
  purpose: "Generate draft clinical discharge summaries from unstructured clinician notes"
  controller: "ExampleHospital NHS Trust"
  dpo: "Dr. Sarah Smith, dpo@hospital.example"

data_flows:
  - id: flow_1
    from: "Clinician (authenticated user)"
    to: "Hospital AI Gateway (internal)"
    data_types:
      - patient_name: special_category_health
      - clinical_notes: special_category_health
      - ward_and_date: personal_data
    legal_basis: "Art.9(2)(h) — medical purposes; Art.6(1)(e) — public task"
    encryption: tls_1_3_in_transit

  - id: flow_2
    from: "Hospital AI Gateway"
    to: "Self-hosted Llama 3 (on-prem GPU cluster)"
    data_types:
      - pseudonymized_notes: special_category_health
    note: "Patient identifiers replaced with pseudonymous tokens before this hop"
    encryption: internal_tls

  - id: flow_3
    from: "Self-hosted Llama 3"
    to: "Hospital AI Gateway"
    data_types:
      - draft_summary: special_category_health
    retention: "Not retained by model; no training use"

  - id: flow_4
    from: "Hospital AI Gateway"
    to: "Clinician (authenticated user)"
    data_types:
      - draft_summary_re_identified: special_category_health
    note: "Pseudonymous tokens replaced with real identifiers for clinician review"

  - id: flow_5
    from: "Hospital AI Gateway"
    to: "Audit Log Store (internal)"
    data_types:
      - interaction_metadata: personal_data
      - prompt_hash: non_personal
    retention_days: 90

The French CNIL published a set of DPIA recommendations specific to AI systems in 2023, following an investigation into several commercial GenAI services. The CNIL identified that most commercial DPIA templates had not been updated to address the following AI-specific gaps: the absence of a data flow step documenting what the model provider does with prompt data, no risk entry for hallucination producing false information about individuals, and no assessment of whether the AI system triggers automated decision-making rights under Article 22.

Organizations that had followed the CNIL's updated template were able to demonstrate that these risks had been identified and mitigated. Organizations using generic software DPIA templates without AI-specific sections faced enforcement action or supervisory inquiry.
Using a generic software DPIA template without GenAI-specific risk categories:
Standard software DPIA templates do not include hallucination risk, training data leakage, or prompt injection as risk categories
Omitting the model provider from the data flow:
The model API provider is a data processor under GDPR; their handling of prompt data must be documented and governed by a Data Processing Agreement
Any AI system whose output materially influences a decision about an individual must be assessed for Article 22 compliance
Assigning risk scores without documenting the reasoning makes the assessment impossible to challenge, update, or defend
Treating the DPIA as a point-in-time document:
A DPIA must be updated when the system changes materially; a DPIA completed at launch that has not been updated in two years is legally insufficient for a significantly evolved system
Pre-populate the risk register with GenAI-specific risk categories so assessors do not need to identify them from scratch
Require a data flow diagram as a mandatory DPIA artifact; the diagram should show every system that handles personal data including the model API provider
Include the model provider's data handling obligations in the Data Processing Agreement and reference the DPA in the DPIA
Document risk scoring reasoning, not just scores, so the assessment can be updated and reviewed by future DPOs
Update the DPIA whenever the system changes materially: new data sources, model changes, expanded user populations, new use cases
Engage the DPO throughout the process, not only at sign-off; DPO input during risk identification produces better mitigations

Completing a thorough DPIA for a complex GenAI system can take four to eight weeks. Most of that time is spent on data flow mapping (often the hardest part because AI systems touch many data stores and external APIs) and on the cross-functional review cycles. Maintaining a library of reusable DPIA components — pre-drafted lawful basis assessments for common scenarios, a standard risk register for the organization's common AI use cases, and approved mitigation boilerplate — can reduce the drafting time significantly while maintaining quality.
The French CNIL publishes a free, open-source DPIA software tool that implements the GDPR Article 35 structure and includes a risk catalog that can be extended with GenAI-specific categories
Commercial platform with DPIA workflow, data mapping integration, and DPO review features
The European Data Protection Board's Guidelines 04/2019 on Article 35 and WP248 (Article 29 Working Party guidelines on DPIAs) are the authoritative reference documents
The international standard for privacy impact assessments provides additional methodology guidance compatible with GDPR Article 35

The completed DPIA is a sensitive document: it catalogues the risks associated with a specific system, including security vulnerabilities that have been mitigated but not eliminated. Store DPIAs in a controlled-access document management system. Do not circulate them via unmanaged email, and do not include them in responses to data subject access requests (they are internal management documents, not records of personal data about the requester).

The risk register within the DPIA should be treated as a living document and reviewed at least annually. If a risk materializes as an incident, the DPIA must be updated to reflect what the residual risk actually was and how the incident response is changing the mitigation controls.

A GenAI DPIA must extend the standard four-section GDPR structure with AI-specific risk categories: hallucination, training data leakage, prompt injection, automated decision-making, and discriminatory outputs
The data flow description must document the model API provider as a data processor and include their contractual obligations regarding prompt data handling
Risk scoring must include reasoning, not just scores, so the assessment can be updated and defended by future DPOs
The model provider's Data Processing Agreement is a required mitigation for the risk of unauthorized access to prompt data
Article 22 applicability must be assessed for any GenAI system whose output materially influences decisions about individuals
The DPIA is a living document: update it whenever the system changes materially, and review it annually at minimum
Where residual risks remain high after mitigation, GDPR Article 36 requires prior consultation with the supervisory authority
